title: THE FAITH
subtitle: Why we're like this. An explainer for the uninitiated.

beats.title: In the beginning
beats.text: The Knicks are a charter member of the league. Before the NBA was the NBA, New York was already in the building. The building came later. The attitude was always there.

beats.title: The Captain walks
beats.text: Willis Reed tears a muscle in his thigh, misses Game 6 of the Finals, and then walks out of the tunnel for Game 7 anyway. He hits his first two shots. Clyde Frazier drops 36 and 19. First title. If you learn one Knicks story, learn this one, it's the template: pain first, then glory.

beats.title: Do it again
beats.text: Second championship. Red Holzman's beautiful team basketball, hit the open man, see the ball. New York assumes this will happen regularly forever. New York is about to learn something.

beats.title: So close it still hurts
beats.text: Ewing, Starks, Oakley. A Finals that goes seven games against Houston. Starks goes 2-for-18 in Game 7. We don't blame him, he got us there. But somewhere in every Knicks fan's chest there is a small room with 1994 written on the door, and we do not open it.

beats.title: Chaos run
beats.text: An 8-seed reaches the Finals, still the only one ever. Larry Johnson's four-point play against Indiana is the loudest the Garden has ever been about a regular human activity. We lose the Finals. It doesn't matter. It was glorious.

beats.title: The wilderness
beats.text: Here's the part outsiders never get. The teams were bad. Historically bad. Owner drama, coaching carousels, trades we don't discuss. And the Garden SOLD OUT. Seventeen straight years. Loving this team through this era wasn't fandom, it was a covenant. You don't choose the Knicks because they win. You choose them because they're yours.

beats.title: Two weeks of Linsanity
beats.text: An undrafted guard sleeping on a teammate's couch drops 38 on the Lakers and for two weeks the whole planet watches Knicks basketball. It ends as fast as it starts. Classic us: even our miracles had a expiration date. Until.

beats.title: Bing bong
beats.text: The Knicks make the playoffs for the first time in 8 years. A fan yells 'bing bong' into a news camera and it becomes the city's national anthem. This is important context: New York was SO ready. The kindling was stacked 50 years high.

beats.title: The quiet signing
beats.text: Dallas lets a 6'2" backup point guard walk in free agency. Leon Rose, who has known the Brunson family forever, brings him to New York. Nobody throws a parade. The parade is scheduled for later.

beats.title: The hard call
beats.text: The Knicks lose the East finals to Indiana and fire the coach who got them there. Ruthless? Maybe. But 53 years teaches you the difference between good and good enough. Mike Brown, fired by Sacramento 18 months earlier, gets the job. Two rejects with something to prove. Extremely New York.

beats.title: The last test
beats.text: Nine losses in eleven games. Talk radio wants everyone traded, fired, or both. 'Possession over outcome,' Brown keeps saying. The city rolls its eyes and shows up anyway. It always shows up anyway. That's the whole point.

beats.title: Everything, all at once
beats.text: Down 2-1 to Atlanta, then 13 straight playoff wins. A 22-point comeback buries Cleveland. Then the Finals: down big in every win and coming back every time, like the team was performing 53 years of fan experience in real time. Anunoby's tip-in with 2.1 seconds left. And in Game 5, in San Antonio, Jalen Brunson, the 33rd pick, scores 45, ties Michael Jordan, and cries into his father's shoulder.

beats.title: Why we're fans
beats.text: Because the Garden sits on top of a train station and 20,000 strangers become one organism there. Because we chanted DE-FENSE first. Because we sold out the bad years. Because Willis walked so Brunson could dance. You don't love the Knicks because it's easy. You love the Knicks because when it finally, FINALLY pays off, nothing on earth feels like this. Bing bong.
